title: Quiz: Limitations of Hartree-Fock
description: Test your understanding of HF limitations and when post-HF methods are needed

questions.question: What is electron correlation energy?
questions.explanation: Correlation energy is defined as E_corr = E_exact - E_HF. Since HF always gives an energy that is too high (variational principle), correlation energy is always negative.

questions.question: What happens to RHF energy when H₂ is dissociated to large separation?

options.text: It is too high due to ionic contamination

questions.explanation: At dissociation, RHF incorrectly gives 50% ionic (H⁺ + H⁻) character instead of 100% neutral atoms (H• + H•), making the energy much too high.

questions.question: What is the main approximation in Hartree-Fock theory?
questions.explanation: The fundamental HF approximation is the mean-field approximation: each electron sees only the average field of all other electrons, not their instantaneous positions.

questions.question: Which type of correlation is associated with near-degeneracy of electronic configurations?
questions.explanation: Static correlation arises when multiple electronic configurations are nearly degenerate and must be treated on equal footing. This occurs in bond breaking, diradicals, and transition metals.

questions.question: HF can correctly describe dispersion interactions between molecules.
questions.explanation: False. Dispersion (van der Waals) interactions are purely a correlation effect. HF completely misses dispersion since it lacks electron correlation.

questions.question: What is the typical error in HF reaction barriers?

options.text: HF barriers are too low by 5-10 kcal/mol
HF barriers are too high by 5-10 kcal/mol
HF cannot predict barriers at all

questions.explanation: HF systematically overestimates reaction barriers because transition states typically have more correlation energy than reactants or products.

questions.question: Which molecule is actually UNBOUND at the HF level?
questions.explanation: F₂ is unbound at the HF level—it has negative atomization energy! This dramatic failure illustrates the importance of correlation for weak bonds.

questions.question: Which properties does HF predict reasonably well? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: HF predicts equilibrium geometries (within 0.01-0.02 Å) and dipole moments (within 5-10%) reasonably well. Atomization energies have large errors, and dispersion is completely missing.
